reasoning: The initial observation shows that when mass is 10 kg, acceleration is 2 m/s², and force is 20 N. Using F = ma, we can verify if this satisfies the equation. Next, I will test how changing the mass affects the force while keeping acceleration constant.

The current observation confirms the initial hypothesis that force is equal to mass times acceleration (F = ma). Now, I will test how increasing or decreasing the acceleration affects the force while keeping the mass constant at 20 kg.

The observation shows that when mass is 20 kg and acceleration increases from 2 m/s² to 3 m/s², the force also increases from 40 N to 60 N. This further supports the hypothesis that force is directly proportional to both mass and acceleration. Next, I should test how decreasing the acceleration affects the force while keeping the mass constant.

The observations show that when mass is 20 kg and acceleration changes, the force changes proportionally. To solidify the hypothesis, I will now test how decreasing the mass affects the force while keeping the acceleration constant.

The previous steps have shown that the force (F) is consistently equal to the product of mass (m) and acceleration (a), as confirmed by the equation F = ma. Now, I need to test how varying both mass and acceleration independently affects the force. I will start by testing a smaller mass and a larger acceleration to see if the force increases.

The last observation showed that when mass is reduced to 0 kg, the force becomes 0 N regardless of the acceleration. This suggests that the equation F = ma holds true for any non-zero mass and acceleration. Since the hypothesis has been verified through multiple scenarios, it is now time to conclude that the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration is indeed F = ma.
